items.subtopic: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature | NORMAL BODY TEMPERATURES
items.title: Defining Fever: Clinical Thresholds and Influencing Factors
items.content: Fever is defined as a body temperature exceeding the 99th percentile for healthy individuals, which is >37.7°C (>99.9°F). This threshold accounts for normal variations in baseline temperatures influenced by age, race, sex, ambient conditions, and comorbidities.
keyPoints: Normal core body temperature range: 36.5-37.5°C (97.7-99.5°F)
Mean oral temperature in adults: 36.6°C (95% CI, 35.7–37.3°C)
Fever threshold: >37.7°C (>99.9°F), representing the 99th percentile
Influences on baseline body temperature include age, race/sex demographics, and comorbid conditions like cancer (+0.02°C) or hypothyroidism (-0.01°C)
An increase of 0.15°C in baseline temperature correlates with a 0.52% absolute increase in 1-year mortality
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature | NORMAL BODY TEMPERATURES

items.subtopic: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature | NORMAL BODY TEMPERATURES
items.title: Comparing Thermometry Methods: Accuracy and Variability
items.content: Different thermometry methods vary in accuracy and consistency. Rectal temperatures are generally higher than oral readings by approximately 0.4°C (0.7°F). Tympanic membrane thermometers, especially in unadjusted mode, can be less reliable, showing readings ~0.8°C (1.6°F) lower than rectal measurements.
keyPoints: Rectal temperatures are typically 0.4°C higher than oral
Tympanic thermometer readings: Lower by ~0.8°C compared to rectal in unadjusted mode
Oral readings may be affected by mouth breathing, especially in respiratory infections or rapid breathing
Lower-esophageal temperatures closely reflect core body temperature
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature | NORMAL BODY TEMPERATURES

items.subtopic: FEVER VERSUS HYPERTHERMIA
items.title: Mechanisms Differentiating Fever from Hyperthermia
items.content: Fever involves a hypothalamic set point increase, leading to vasoconstriction and heat conservation mechanisms until the new temperature is reached. In contrast, hyperthermia results from an uncontrolled rise in body temperature without changes in the hypothalmic set point.
keyPoints: Fever: Hypothalamic set point increased (e.g., 37°C to 39°C), vasoconstriction, and heat conservation mechanisms activated.
Hyperthermia: Uncontrolled increase in body temperature due to exogenous or endogenous factors without hypothalamic set point change.
Distinguishing feature: Hyperthermia does not respond to antipyretics; fever usually does.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · FEVER VERSUS HYPERTHERMIA

items.subtopic: FEVER VERSUS HYPERTHERMIA
items.title: Clinical Presentation and Diagnosis of Fever vs. Hyperthermia
items.content: Fever often presents with vasoconstriction (cold extremities) and can be reduced by antipyretics, while hyperthermia presents with hot, dry skin and does not respond to these medications.
keyPoints: Fever: Cold skin due to vasoconstriction; responds to antipyretics like aspirin or acetaminophen.
Hyperthermia: Hot, dry skin; no response to antipyretics.
Diagnostic clue for hyperthermia includes recent heat exposure or drug interference with thermoregulation.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · FEVER VERSUS HYPERTHERMIA

items.subtopic: FEVER VERSUS HYPERTHERMIA
items.title: Critical Thresholds and Risks in Fever and Hyperthermia
items.content: A fever exceeding 41.5°C (106.7°F) is termed hyperpyrexia, which can be life-threatening and often requires differentiation from hyperthermia.
keyPoints: Hyperpyrexia: Body temperature >41.5°C (>106.7°F), potentially life-threatening.
Common in severe infections or CNS hemorrhages; historically rarely exceeded 106°F due to natural thermal ceiling.
Hyperthermia can be rapidly fatal and does not respond to antipyretics.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · FEVER VERSUS HYPERTHERMIA

items.subtopic: PYROGENS | PYROGENIC CYTOKINES
items.title: Understanding Pyrogenic Cytokines and Their Role in Fever Production
items.content: Pyrogenic cytokines, including IL-1, IL-6, TNF, and ciliary neurotropic factor, are small proteins that regulate immune responses and induce fever. They act as endogenous pyrogens by triggering the hypothalamus to increase body temperature set points.
keyPoints: Cytokines like IL-1, IL-6, and TNF cause fever by acting on the hypothalamus.
IL-1 and TNF induce fever at doses of 10–100 ng/kg in humans.
IL-6 requires a higher dose (1–10 μg/kg) to produce fever.
Pyrogenic cytokines are involved in both infectious and non-infectious inflammatory processes.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · PYROGENS | PYROGENIC CYTOKINES

items.subtopic: PYROGENS | PYROGENIC CYTOKINES
items.title: Dose-Dependent Fever Induction by Exogenous Pyrogens
items.content: Exogenous pyrogens such as endotoxins from gram-negative bacteria and superantigens from staphylococci and streptococci can induce fever. Endotoxin is highly pyrogenic, causing fever at doses of 2–3 ng/kg in humans.
keyPoints: Endotoxin (lipopolysaccharide) causes fever at 2–3 ng/kg in humans.
Superantigens from staphylococci and streptococci induce fever at 1–10 μg/kg in experimental animals.
Exogenous pyrogens include microbial products, toxins, and whole microorganisms.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · PYROGENS | PYROGENIC CYTOKINES

items.subtopic: ELEVATION OF THE HYPOTHALAMIC SET POINT BY CYTOKINES
items.title: Mechanism of Fever Induction by Pyrogenic Cytokines
items.content: Pyrogenic cytokines such as IL-1, IL-6, and TNF are released from myeloid and endothelial cells into the systemic circulation. These cytokines induce the synthesis of prostaglandin E2 (PGE₂) both systemically and in hypothalamic tissue, leading to fever by raising the hypothalamic set point.
keyPoints: Pyrogenic cytokines interact with endothelium near circumventricular organs to initiate fever.
Circulating PGE₂ is essential for increasing hypothalamic set point.
EP-3 receptor on glial cells is crucial for fever response; its deletion prevents fever induction by IL-1 or endotoxin.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · ELEVATION OF THE HYPOTHALAMIC SET POINT BY CYTOKINES

items.subtopic: ELEVATION OF THE HYPOTHALAMIC SET POINT BY CYTOKINES
items.title: Role of PGE₂ and Receptors in Fever Pathophysiology
items.content: PGE₂, elevated during fever, acts on the EP-3 receptor to stimulate glial cells, leading to cAMP release. This process activates neuronal pathways in the hypothalamus, altering the set point for body temperature.
keyPoints: PGE₂ is not a neurotransmitter but triggers cAMP release from glial cells.
cAMP acts as a neurotransmitter, influencing thermoregulatory neurons.
Toll-like receptors on hypothalamic endothelium can directly activate PGE₂ production and fever via shared signaling with IL-1 receptors.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · ELEVATION OF THE HYPOTHALAMIC SET POINT BY CYTOKINES

items.subtopic: PRODUCTION OF CYTOKINES IN THE CNS
items.title: CNS Cytokines and Hyperpyrexia: Mechanisms and Implications
items.content: Cytokines produced within the central nervous system (CNS) can lead to hyperpyrexia by directly influencing the hypothalamic set point. This mechanism is particularly relevant in conditions such as CNS hemorrhage, trauma, or infection. The production of cytokines like IL-1, TNF, and IL-6 by microglial cells and possibly neurons during viral infections of the CNS can induce fever more effectively when these cytokines are present within the brain substance or ventricles compared to systemic circulation.
keyPoints: Cytokines produced in the CNS can cause hyperpyrexia by raising the hypothalamic set point, bypassing circumventricular organs.
Concentration of a cytokine required to cause fever is significantly lower with direct injection into the brain substance or ventricles than with systemic injection.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · PRODUCTION OF CYTOKINES IN THE CNS | HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY TESTS

items.subtopic: HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAMINATION
items.title: Fever in Special Populations: Recognizing Blunted Febrile Responses
items.content: Certain populations, including newborns, elderly patients, those with chronic hepatic or renal failure, and individuals on glucocorticoids or anticytokine therapy, may exhibit a blunted febrile response. This can result in active disease without the presence of fever, necessitating careful clinical assessment beyond temperature measurements alone.
keyPoints: Newborns, elderly patients, those with chronic hepatic or renal failure, and patients on glucocorticoids or anticytokine therapy may not exhibit fever despite active disease due to a blunted febrile response.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · PRODUCTION OF CYTOKINES IN THE CNS | HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY TESTS

items.subtopic: FEVER IN PATIENTS RECEIVING ANTICYTOKINE THERAPY | THE DECISION TO TREAT FEVER
items.title: Recognizing Blunted Febrile Response in Anticytokine Therapy Patients
items.content: Patients on anticytokine therapy may exhibit a blunted febrile response to infections due to the suppression of cytokines like IL-1, IL-6, IL-12/23, IL-17, and TNF. This can mask signs of infection such as fever, necessitating early and rigorous diagnostic evaluation.
keyPoints: Anticytokine therapy lowers host defenses against infections including M. tuberculosis and fungal infections.
Febrile response may be blunted in patients on anticytokine therapies, glucocorticoids, or NSAIDs.
Low-grade fever is concerning; early diagnostic evaluation is crucial.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · FEVER IN PATIENTS RECEIVING ANTICYTOKINE THERAPY | THE DECISION TO TREAT FEVER

items.subtopic: FEVER IN PATIENTS RECEIVING ANTICYTOKINE THERAPY | THE DECISION TO TREAT FEVER
items.title: When to Withhold Antipyretics in Bacterial Infections
items.content: In bacterial infections, withholding antipyretic therapy can help evaluate the effectiveness of antibiotics, especially when cultures are negative. This approach may reveal inadequately treated infections and assist in diagnosing unusual febrile diseases.
keyPoints: Antipyretics do not delay resolution of viral or bacterial infections.
Withholding antipyretics can aid in assessing antibiotic effectiveness.
Temperature-pulse dissociation is a diagnostic clue for certain infections.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · FEVER IN PATIENTS RECEIVING ANTICYTOKINE THERAPY | THE DECISION TO TREAT FEVER

items.subtopic: FEVER IN PATIENTS RECEIVING ANTICYTOKINE THERAPY | THE DECISION TO TREAT FEVER
items.title: Characteristic Fever Patterns in Specific Infections
items.content: Certain infections exhibit characteristic fever patterns, such as Plasmodium vivax (every third day) and Plasmodium malariae (every fourth day). Recognizing these patterns aids in diagnosis and management.
keyPoints: Plasmodium vivax causes fever every third day; Plasmodium malariae every fourth day.
Pel-Ebstein pattern: fever 3–10 days, afebrile 3–10 days, associated with Hodgkin disease.
Cyclic neutropenia: fevers occur every 21 days.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · FEVER IN PATIENTS RECEIVING ANTICYTOKINE THERAPY | THE DECISION TO TREAT FEVER

items.subtopic: ANTICYTOKINE THERAPY TO REDUCE FEVER IN AUTOIMMUNE AND AUTOINFLAMMATORY DISEASES
items.title: Role of IL-1 Blockade in Reducing Fever in Autoinflammatory Diseases
items.content: In autoinflammatory diseases, recurrent fevers are a hallmark and are mediated by IL-1β. Blocking IL-1 activity with agents like anakinra or canakinumab significantly reduces these fevers. This therapeutic approach is effective across both uncommon (e.g., familial Mediterranean fever) and common conditions (e.g., idiopathic pericarditis).
keyPoints: Recurrent fevers are characteristic of autoinflammatory diseases.
IL-1β mediates the fevers in these diseases.
Anakinra or canakinumab blocks IL-1 activity, reducing fever significantly.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · ANTICYTOKINE THERAPY TO REDUCE FEVER IN AUTOIMMUNE AND AUTOINFLAMMATORY DISEASES | MECHANISMS OF ANTIPYRETIC AGENTS

items.subtopic: MECHANISMS OF ANTIPYRETIC AGENTS
items.title: Mechanism of Action: Antipyretics Lower Hypothalamic Set Point via PGE2 Inhibition
items.content: Antipyretic agents reduce fever by lowering the hypothalamic set point, primarily through inhibition of prostaglandin E2 (PGE2) synthesis. Cyclooxygenase inhibitors like NSAIDs and acetaminophen are potent antipyretics due to their ability to inhibit cyclooxygenase activity in the brain. Acetaminophen's unique action involves COX-3 inhibition, which is specific to the CNS.
keyPoints: Fever reduction occurs by lowering the hypothalamic set point via PGE2 level reduction.
Cyclooxygenase inhibitors are potent antipyretics due to their effect on brain cyclooxygenase.
Acetaminophen inhibits COX-3 in the CNS, contributing to its antipyretic effect.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · ANTICYTOKINE THERAPY TO REDUCE FEVER IN AUTOIMMUNE AND AUTOINFLAMMATORY DISEASES | MECHANISMS OF ANTIPYRETIC AGENTS

items.subtopic: REGIMENS FOR THE TREATMENT OF FEVER
items.title: Why Acetaminophen is Preferred Over Aspirin and NSAIDs in Fever Management
items.content: Acetaminophen is preferred as an antipyretic over aspirin and NSAIDs due to its lower risk of adverse effects on platelets and the gastrointestinal tract. In children, acetaminophen or oral ibuprofen should be used instead of aspirin because of the increased risk of Reye syndrome associated with aspirin use in certain viral infections.
keyPoints: Acetaminophen is preferred due to lower risks on platelets and GI tract compared to NSAIDs and aspirin.
Aspirin increases the risk of Reye syndrome in children with certain viral infections.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · REGIMENS FOR THE TREATMENT OF FEVER

items.subtopic: REGIMENS FOR THE TREATMENT OF FEVER
items.title: When Aggressive Fever Management is Indicated
items.content: Aggressive fever management is recommended for patients with preexisting cardiac, pulmonary, or CNS impairment due to increased oxygen demand and potential exacerbation of their condition. It is also advised in children with a history of febrile seizures, although the exact trigger for these seizures remains unclear.
keyPoints: Fever increases oxygen consumption by 13% per $1^{	ext{o}}$C increase over $37^{	ext{o}}$C.
Aggressive fever management is recommended in patients with preexisting cardiac, pulmonary, or CNS impairment.
Children with a history of febrile seizures should have their fever aggressively managed.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · REGIMENS FOR THE TREATMENT OF FEVER

items.subtopic: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature | NORMAL BODY TEMPERATURES
items.question: Which factor is associated with a higher baseline body temperature?
items.explanation: Higher ambient temperatures are linked to higher baseline body temperatures.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature | NORMAL BODY TEMPERATURES

items.subtopic: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature | NORMAL BODY TEMPERATURES
items.question: What is the mean oral temperature according to a study of >35,000 individuals?
items.explanation: The mean oral temperature is 36.6°C with a 95% confidence interval of 35.7–37.3°C.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature | NORMAL BODY TEMPERATURES

items.subtopic: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature | NORMAL BODY TEMPERATURES
items.question: What is the typical difference between rectal and oral temperatures?
items.explanation: Rectal temperatures are generally 0.4°C higher than oral readings.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature | NORMAL BODY TEMPERATURES

items.subtopic: FEVER VERSUS HYPERTHERMIA
items.question: Which mechanism is primarily responsible for the initial sensation of cold during a fever?
options: Vasoconstriction decreases heat loss from the skin.
Increased nonshivering thermogenesis in the liver.
Behavioral adjustments like putting on more clothing.
items.explanation: The initial sensation of cold during a fever is due to vasoconstriction, which shunts blood away from the periphery and decreases heat loss from the skin.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · FEVER VERSUS HYPERTHERMIA

items.subtopic: FEVER VERSUS HYPERTHERMIA
items.question: What typically happens when the hypothalamic set point is reset downward in response to antipyretics?
options: Heat loss through vasodilation and sweating occurs.
items.explanation: When the hypothalamic set point is reset downward, heat loss mechanisms such as vasodilation and sweating are initiated to lower body temperature.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · FEVER VERSUS HYPERTHERMIA

items.subtopic: FEVER VERSUS HYPERTHERMIA
items.question: What distinguishes hyperthermia from fever in terms of the hypothalamic set point?
options: The hypothalamic set point is elevated.
The hypothalamic set point remains unchanged.
The hypothalamic set point is lowered.
The hypothalamic set point fluctuates rapidly.
items.explanation: In hyperthermia, the hypothalamic thermoregulatory center's set point remains unchanged, unlike in fever where it is elevated.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · FEVER VERSUS HYPERTHERMIA

items.subtopic: FEVER VERSUS HYPERTHERMIA
items.question: Which of the following conditions is most commonly associated with hyperpyrexia?
options: Subnormal body temperatures due to hypothalamic damage.
Heat stroke from exogenous heat exposure.
items.explanation: Hyperpyrexia, an extraordinarily high fever, most commonly occurs in patients with central nervous system hemorrhages.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · FEVER VERSUS HYPERTHERMIA

items.subtopic: PYROGENS | PYROGENIC CYTOKINES
items.question: Which of the following is a classic example of an exogenous pyrogen?
items.explanation: Lipopolysaccharide, or endotoxin, produced by gram-negative bacteria is a classic example of an exogenous pyrogen.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · PYROGENS | PYROGENIC CYTOKINES

items.subtopic: PYROGENS | PYROGENIC CYTOKINES
items.question: Which cytokine requires the highest dose to induce fever when injected into humans?
items.explanation: Interleukin-6 requires a dose of 1–10 μg/kg to induce fever, which is higher than the doses required for IL-1 and TNF.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · PYROGENS | PYROGENIC CYTOKINES

items.subtopic: PYROGENS | PYROGENIC CYTOKINES
items.question: Which of the following processes can induce the production of pyrogenic cytokines in the absence of microbial infection?
items.explanation: Routine immunizations can induce the production of pyrogenic cytokines such as IL-1, TNF, and/or IL-6 without microbial infection.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · PYROGENS | PYROGENIC CYTOKINES

items.subtopic: ELEVATION OF THE HYPOTHALAMIC SET POINT BY CYTOKINES
items.question: Which receptor for PGE2 is essential for the induction of fever?
items.explanation: The EP-3 receptor is essential for fever; deletion of its gene prevents fever after IL-1 or endotoxin injection.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · ELEVATION OF THE HYPOTHALAMIC SET POINT BY CYTOKINES

items.subtopic: ELEVATION OF THE HYPOTHALAMIC SET POINT BY CYTOKINES
items.question: Where are the concentrations of PGE2 highest during fever?
items.explanation: PGE2 concentrations are highest near the circumventricular vascular organs surrounding hypothalamic regulatory centers.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · ELEVATION OF THE HYPOTHALAMIC SET POINT BY CYTOKINES

items.subtopic: ELEVATION OF THE HYPOTHALAMIC SET POINT BY CYTOKINES
items.question: Which of the following statements about PGE2 is true?
options: PGE2 induces cAMP release from glial cells
PGE2 receptors are located on muscle cells
items.explanation: PGE2 triggers the PGE2 receptor on glial cells, leading to rapid cAMP release.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · ELEVATION OF THE HYPOTHALAMIC SET POINT BY CYTOKINES

items.subtopic: PRODUCTION OF CYTOKINES IN THE CNS | HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY TESTS
items.question: Which cytokines are produced by microglial and possibly neuronal cells in response to viral infections of the CNS?
items.explanation: Viral infections of the CNS induce microglial and possibly neuronal production of IL-1, TNF, and IL-6.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · PRODUCTION OF CYTOKINES IN THE CNS | HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY TESTS

items.subtopic: PRODUCTION OF CYTOKINES IN THE CNS | HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY TESTS
items.question: Why are cytokines produced in the CNS more effective at causing fever than those injected systemically?
options: They require a higher concentration to cause fever.
They can raise the hypothalamic set point directly.
They interact with peripheral immune cells.
items.explanation: Cytokines produced in the CNS can raise the hypothalamic set point, bypassing the circumventricular organs.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · PRODUCTION OF CYTOKINES IN THE CNS | HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY TESTS

items.question: Which patients may not exhibit fever despite having active disease due to a blunted febrile response?
options: Newborns, elderly patients, and those on glucocorticoids or anticytokine treatment.
All patients with chronic hepatic failure.
items.explanation: Physicians should be aware that newborns, elderly patients, patients with chronic hepatic or renal failure, and patients taking glucocorticoids or being treated with an anticytokine may have active disease in the absence of fever because of a blunted febrile response.

Acetaminophen and ibuprofen are more effective than aspirin.

items.statement: Interleukin-6 (IL-6) is less pyrogenic than IL-1 and TNF.

items.subtopic: ELEVATION OF THE HYPOTHALAMIC SET POINT BY CYTOKINES
items.statement: Pyrogenic cytokines pass from the circulation into the brain itself.
items.explanation: Most studies in animals have failed to show that pyrogenic cytokines pass directly from the circulation into the brain.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · ELEVATION OF THE HYPOTHALAMIC SET POINT BY CYTOKINES

items.subtopic: ELEVATION OF THE HYPOTHALAMIC SET POINT BY CYTOKINES
items.explanation: PGE2 is not a neurotransmitter; it triggers cAMP release, which acts as a neurotransmitter.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · ELEVATION OF THE HYPOTHALAMIC SET POINT BY CYTOKINES

items.subtopic: PRODUCTION OF CYTOKINES IN THE CNS | HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY TESTS
items.statement: Measurement of circulating cytokines in patients with fever is typically helpful for diagnosis.
items.explanation: Measurement of circulating cytokines in patients with fever is not helpful since levels often are below the detection limit or do not coincide with fever.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · PRODUCTION OF CYTOKINES IN THE CNS | HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY TESTS

items.subtopic: PRODUCTION OF CYTOKINES IN THE CNS | HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY TESTS
items.statement: CRP levels remain elevated during a febrile disease, even if IL-6 levels vary.
items.explanation: Although IL-6 levels may vary during a febrile disease, CRP levels remain elevated.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · PRODUCTION OF CYTOKINES IN THE CNS | HISTORY AND PHYSICAL EXAMINATION | LABORATORY TESTS

items.subtopic: FEVER IN PATIENTS RECEIVING ANTICYTOKINE THERAPY | THE DECISION TO TREAT FEVER
items.statement: Fever itself is considered an illness in patients receiving anticytokine therapy.
items.explanation: The passage states that fever is not an illness but a response to perturbation of normal host physiology.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · FEVER IN PATIENTS RECEIVING ANTICYTOKINE THERAPY | THE DECISION TO TREAT FEVER

items.subtopic: FEVER IN PATIENTS RECEIVING ANTICYTOKINE THERAPY | THE DECISION TO TREAT FEVER
items.statement: Withholding antipyretic therapy can help evaluate the effectiveness of antibiotics in bacterial infections.
items.explanation: The passage mentions that withholding antipyretics can be helpful in evaluating antibiotic effectiveness, especially without positive cultures.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · FEVER IN PATIENTS RECEIVING ANTICYTOKINE THERAPY | THE DECISION TO TREAT FEVER

items.subtopic: ANTICYTOKINE THERAPY TO REDUCE FEVER IN AUTOIMMUNE AND AUTOINFLAMMATORY DISEASES | MECHANISMS OF ANTIPYRETIC AGENTS
items.statement: COX-3 is found outside the CNS.
items.explanation: COX-3 is not found outside the CNS.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · ANTICYTOKINE THERAPY TO REDUCE FEVER IN AUTOIMMUNE AND AUTOINFLAMMATORY DISEASES | MECHANISMS OF ANTIPYRETIC AGENTS

items.subtopic: ANTICYTOKINE THERAPY TO REDUCE FEVER IN AUTOIMMUNE AND AUTOINFLAMMATORY DISEASES | MECHANISMS OF ANTIPYRETIC AGENTS
items.statement: Chronic, high-dose therapy with antipyretics such as aspirin or any NSAID reduces normal core body temperature.
items.explanation: Chronic, high-dose therapy with antipyretics does not reduce normal core body temperature.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · ANTICYTOKINE THERAPY TO REDUCE FEVER IN AUTOIMMUNE AND AUTOINFLAMMATORY DISEASES | MECHANISMS OF ANTIPYRETIC AGENTS

items.subtopic: REGIMENS FOR THE TREATMENT OF FEVER
items.statement: There is a correlation between absolute temperature elevation and onset of a febrile seizure in susceptible children.
items.explanation: There is no correlation between absolute temperature elevation and the onset of a febrile seizure in susceptible children.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · REGIMENS FOR THE TREATMENT OF FEVER

items.subtopic: REGIMENS FOR THE TREATMENT OF FEVER
items.statement: Cooling blankets should be used without oral antipyretics to effectively reduce fever.
items.explanation: Cooling blankets should not be used without oral antipyretics.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · REGIMENS FOR THE TREATMENT OF FEVER

items.subtopic: FEVER VERSUS HYPERTHERMIA
items.reason: Hyperthermia does not respond to antipyretics and involves an uncontrolled increase in body temperature.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why hyperthermia can be rapidly fatal.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · FEVER VERSUS HYPERTHERMIA

items.subtopic: FEVER VERSUS HYPERTHERMIA
items.assertion: Hyperpyrexia is a term used to describe fevers exceeding 41.5°C.
items.reason: Hyperpyrexia commonly occurs in patients with severe infections and CNS hemorrhages.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why hyperpyrexia is associated with certain conditions.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · FEVER VERSUS HYPERTHERMIA

items.subtopic: FEVER IN PATIENTS RECEIVING ANTICYTOKINE THERAPY | THE DECISION TO TREAT FEVER
items.assertion: The febrile response is blunted in patients receiving chronic glucocorticoid therapy.
items.reason: Chronic glucocorticoid therapy reduces inflammation and immune responses.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why the febrile response is blunted.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · FEVER IN PATIENTS RECEIVING ANTICYTOKINE THERAPY | THE DECISION TO TREAT FEVER

items.subtopic: FEVER IN PATIENTS RECEIVING ANTICYTOKINE THERAPY | THE DECISION TO TREAT FEVER
items.assertion: Plasmodium malariae causes fever every third day.
items.reason: The periodicity of febrile episodes is characteristic of different Plasmodium species.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; Plasmodium malariae causes fever every fourth day, not every third.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · FEVER IN PATIENTS RECEIVING ANTICYTOKINE THERAPY | THE DECISION TO TREAT FEVER

items.subtopic: ANTICYTOKINE THERAPY TO REDUCE FEVER IN AUTOIMMUNE AND AUTOINFLAMMATORY DISEASES | MECHANISMS OF ANTIPYRETIC AGENTS
items.reason: They inhibit the activity of phospholipase A2, which is needed to release arachidonic acid from the cell membrane.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · ANTICYTOKINE THERAPY TO REDUCE FEVER IN AUTOIMMUNE AND AUTOINFLAMMATORY DISEASES | MECHANISMS OF ANTIPYRETIC AGENTS

items.subtopic: REGIMENS FOR THE TREATMENT OF FEVER
items.assertion: Fever increases the demand for oxygen.
items.reason: For every increase of $1^{	ext{o}}$C over $37^{	ext{o}}$C, there is a 13% increase in oxygen consumption.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why fever increases the demand for oxygen.
items.reference: Section 2 Alterations in Body Temperature · REGIMENS FOR THE TREATMENT OF FEVER
